figures.gloss: The supreme sky-father and creator deity of Tenerife, who sustains the universe. He is benevolent, remote, and invoked on mountain peaks during times of drought. He famously freed the sun from the volcano's depths.
figures.facet: He represents an active, responsive sky who intervenes forcefully to save the cosmos from volcanic annihilation, but otherwise remains in the high celestial sphere, requiring mediation by rain-priestesses.
figures.provenance: Spanish chroniclers like Fray Alonso de Espinosa (1594); oral traditions recorded after the Castilian conquest.

figures.gloss: The malevolent volcanic entity who resides within Echeyde (Mount Teide). A destructive force of fire and sulfur, he abducted the sun, Magec, and plunged the world into darkness until defeated by Achamán.
figures.facet: Guayota is not a fallen angel or a god of the dead, but the literal, geographical threat of catastrophic volcanism personified—a localized geological terror trapped within the island's central peak.
figures.provenance: Espinosa (1594); Torriani; archaeological finds of clay offerings hidden in volcanic tubes to appease him.

figures.gloss: The supreme solar deity, providing light, warmth, and agricultural fertility. Revered deeply across the islands, their capture by Guayota explains eclipses or volcanic winter, while their liberation restores the cosmic order.
figures.facet: Magec's power is supreme but physically vulnerable to being swallowed by the earth. The sun is not an untouchable celestial chariot, but a captive prize in the war between sky and volcano.
figures.provenance: Chronicler Abreu Galindo (1632); linguistic traces in Guanche oaths swearing 'by the light of Magec'.

figures.gloss: The 'Sun Mother' or 'She who bears the world', a supreme mother goddess of Tenerife. She anchors the agricultural cycle and maternal protection, famously appearing as an icon on the beaches of Güímar before the conquest.
figures.facet: Her worship seamlessly integrated a miraculously washed-up wooden statue, allowing her to physically bridge indigenous cosmic mother-worship with the impending arrival of European Marian devotion.
figures.provenance: Espinosa (1594); the highly documented syncretic cult of the Virgin of Candelaria in post-conquest Canarian history.

figures.gloss: Mount Teide, the towering central volcano of Tenerife, revered as the axis of the world and the literal gate to the underworld. It is the earthly prison where Guayota is eternally contained.
figures.facet: Unlike celestial mountains of the gods, Echeyde is an axis of terror—a pressurized infernal cage holding back cosmic darkness, requiring constant placation through hidden offerings in its slopes.
figures.provenance: Toponymic survival ('Teide' derives from Echeyde); writings of Espinosa and Núñez de la Peña.

figures.gloss: Demonic entities or evil spirits taking the form of enormous, shadowy dogs with glowing red eyes. They roam the wild ravines and volcanic badlands at night, attacking travelers and livestock.
figures.facet: They materialize the existential dread of the island's deep, unlit 'barrancos' (ravines). They are not underworld guards, but autonomous agents of the dark, physical manifestations of Guayota's malice.
figures.provenance: Gran Canarian and Tinerfeñan chronicles (e.g., Marín de Cubas); persistent Canarian folklore regarding giant spectral dogs.

figures.gloss: The meticulously mummified remains of Guanche nobility and ancestors. Prepared with pine resin, pumice, and herbs during the 'mirlado' process, they were wrapped in goat skins and placed in inaccessible volcanic caves.
figures.facet: Death was not a departure, but a transition into eternal guardianship. The xaxos physically remained in the island's rocky veins, transforming volcanic caves from barren geology into sacred ancestral catacombs.
figures.provenance: Extensive archaeological discoveries of Guanche mummies (e.g., in the Barranco de Herques) and chroniclers' accounts of the mummification process.

figures.gloss: Consecrated women who lived in isolated communities, tasked with performing vital rain-making rituals. During droughts, they would lead livestock to the sea or sacred mountains, separating mothers from young to make their cries reach the sky.
figures.facet: They commanded the climatic survival of the islands through orchestrated communal weeping—weaponizing the grief of humans and animals alike to compel the distant sky god to send rain.
figures.provenance: Accounts of the conquest of Gran Canaria; Pedro Gómez Escudero and Abreu Galindo.

figures.gloss: The supreme sky-god of the Canarii of Gran Canaria, invoked with libations of milk and branches raised toward the heavens at sacred high places (almogarenes) in times of drought and crisis.
figures.facet: A second island tradition's own supreme sky-father, attested independently of Tenerife's Achamán — evidence the deus-otiosus office was a shared Canarian pattern, not a Tenerife peculiarity, before the conquest flattened the islands into one 'Guanche' religion.
figures.provenance: Fray Juan de Abreu Galindo's 'Historia de la conquista de las siete islas de Canaria' (1632); conquest-era chronicle accounts of Gran Canarian ritual at sacred sites.

figures.gloss: The supreme celestial deity of the Benahoarites of La Palma, associated with the heavens and worshipped at cairns and roques (natural rock formations) rather than temples.
figures.facet: A third island's independent supreme sky-god, more sparsely documented than Achamán or Acoran, but enough to show the office recurring island by island rather than descending from one shared source.
figures.provenance: Abreu Galindo (1632); later ethnographic compilation of La Palma's pre-conquest religious toponymy.

figures.gloss: The god worshipped exclusively by the men of El Hierro, invoked from the cliffs of Bentayca for rain and healing; paired in the sources with a female counterpart worshipped exclusively by the island's women.
figures.facet: El Hierro's cult is the one clearly attested case in the islands of a divine pair split cleanly along the line of who is permitted to worship whom — gender doesn't just color the cult here, it structures who may even approach each god.
figures.provenance: Abreu Galindo's account of El Hierro's pre-conquest religion, the primary surviving source for this pair.

figures.gloss: The goddess worshipped exclusively by the women of El Hierro, a protective divinity invoked alongside, but never jointly with, the men's god Eraoranhan.
figures.facet: Her existence is only ever recorded as half of a pair — no independent myth or shrine survives for Moneiba apart from her structural opposition to Eraoranhan, which is itself the whole content of what the sources preserve.
figures.provenance: Abreu Galindo's account of El Hierro's pre-conquest religion, the primary surviving source for this pair.

figures.gloss: A towering natural rock monolith (Roque Idafe) inside the Caldera de Taburiente on La Palma, feared as a pillar holding up the world; offerings of entrails were made to it accompanied by a chanted exchange asking it not to fall.
figures.facet: Unlike a celestial axis-mundi reached by ascent, Idafe is a load-bearing structural fear made local and physical — the world's stability is not assumed but ritually renegotiated with a specific rock, on a schedule.
figures.provenance: Chronicle accounts (Abreu Galindo) of pre-conquest La Palma cult practice at the Caldera de Taburiente; the rock's continued toponymic and folkloric survival.

figures.gloss: The title given by Espinosa to Tenerife's diviner-seers, consulted by the island's menceyes (chiefs) and credited in the chronicles with foretelling the coming of the Castilian conquerors before their arrival.
figures.facet: The conquest narrative needs its own native prophetic office to make the fall of the islands look foreordained rather than merely overwhelming — Guañameñe is the Guanche tradition's own answer to the omen-before-the-conquest pattern found elsewhere in colonized religions.
figures.provenance: Fray Alonso de Espinosa's 'Historia de Nuestra Señora de Candelaria' (1594), the primary source recording the title and its prophetic function.

figures.gloss: The August harvest and new-year festival of the Guanches, marking the gathering of crops with communal feasting, dancing, and offerings of milk and gofio (roasted grain meal); the best-attested calendrical rite of the pre-conquest islands.
figures.facet: It anchors the entire Guanche ritual year to a single agricultural event — unlike the crisis-triggered rites (rain-making, volcano-placation), Beñesmén is scheduled and communal, the calendar's one guaranteed, joyful fixture.
figures.provenance: Espinosa (1594) and Abreu Galindo's accounts of the August festival; the toponymic and folkloric survival of harvest customs in later Canarian culture.

edges.note: Intra-tradition bond: The supreme celestial creator and his absolute volcanic adversary.
Intra-tradition bond: The demonic entity and the literal volcanic mountain that serves as his eternal prison.
Intra-tradition bond: The force of darkness that historically captured and eclipsed the sun deity.
Intra-tradition bond: The remote sky god and the specialized order of consecrated women required to reach him.
Intra-tradition bond: The master of volcanic evil and his roaming spectral hounds of the ravines.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent the vast, remote, but ultimately benevolent blue sky whose intervention is sought primarily in dire crises.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are absolute cosmic adversaries who actively attempt to plunge the created world into darkness by attacking the light.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are supreme solar deities whose temporary disappearance or capture plunges the world into a state of cosmic terror.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are supreme, life-sustaining mother figures deeply tied to the land who became heavily syncretized with the Virgin Mary.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both intimately link the imagery of monstrous or shadowy dogs with the dangerous, chthonic forces of the underworld.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both represent the deeply venerated dead who are purposefully anchored to the land to act as eternal guardians of the lineage.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both involve strictly female-led rituals designed to entreat the heavens for rain during periods of profound agricultural crisis.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Where Sumeru is the celestial mountain elevating the cosmos, Echeyde is the infernal pillar threatening to erupt and consume it.
Cross-tradition GOLD SEAM: Both are sovereign, high creator gods who established the world but are generally withdrawn, requiring specific ritual intermediaries.
Island-specific supreme sky-fathers of the same broader Canarian religious world, each invoked chiefly in drought and crisis at high sacred places.
A third island's own supreme celestial deity, parallel in office to Tenerife's Achamán though attested by a separate, thinner chronicle tradition.
Gran Canaria's and La Palma's supreme sky-gods fill the same office independently, each documented only for their own island.
The exclusively male and exclusively female supreme deities of El Hierro, together the clearest gendered-cult division recorded in the archipelago.

Both are feared, world-anchoring volcanic or monolithic masses placated by ritual offering lest catastrophe (collapse or eruption) follow — La Palma's sacred rock and Tenerife's volcano as parallel axis-points of dread.

Espinosa records these seers advising the menceyes within the same religious world that invoked Achamán in crisis — the archipelago's prophetic office alongside its supreme sky-father, not a documented joint cult.
Independent calendrical thanksgiving festivals marking a successful harvest with communal feasting and offering, anchoring the ritual year around agricultural gratitude.

The Guimar washed-up statue is documented (Espinosa, 1594) as identified with both the indigenous Sun Mother and, after conquest, with the Virgin of Candelaria, an advocation of Mary — one image, two theologies fused onto it.

dossier.essence: The indigenous religion of the Guanches, the Berber-descended aboriginal people of the Canary Islands, as it stood before and during the Castilian conquest (1402–1496). Reconstructed almost entirely from post-conquest Spanish chronicles and from archaeology, it centered — on Tenerife — on a remote sky-creator (Achamán), a malevolent volcanic power in Mount Teide (Guayota), a solar deity (Magec), a mother-goddess (Chaxiraxi), and an elaborate cult of the mummified ancestral dead.
dossier.cosmos: A high creator sky-god (Achamán on Tenerife; Acorán and other names on the other islands) made and sustains the world and is invoked from mountaintops, above all in drought. Against him stands Guayota, the evil genius of the volcano Echeyde (Teide); in the tradition's central myth he seizes the sun and plunges the world into darkness until Achamán defeats and imprisons him within the peak. The dead did not simply depart — the nobility were mummified and laid in caves, remaining present in the land as venerated ancestors.
dossier.practice: Rites were led at open-air sanctuaries and sacred mountains by priests and by consecrated women, the harimaguadas; during droughts, livestock were driven to sacred places and their young separated from them so that the animals' cries would carry human need up to the sky. Offerings of milk, butter, and pottery were made — some hidden in volcanic tubes to placate Guayota — and the dead of high rank were embalmed with resin, herbs, and hide wrappings (the mirlado process) and interred in inaccessible caves.
dossier.canon: Purely oral in origin and lost as a living tradition; the religion is known only through conquest-era and later chroniclers — Fray Alonso de Espinosa's 'Historia de Nuestra Señora de Candelaria' (1594), Leonardo Torriani, Abreu Galindo, and Marín de Cubas — supplemented by archaeology (mummies, cave sanctuaries, rock engravings).
dossier.reflects: A religion shaped by a volcanic homeland — a cosmos whose central sacred drama is the sky-god's restraint of a literal mountain of fire, and whose dead are kept, preserved, within the island's own stone.
sources: Alonso de Espinosa, 'Historia de Nuestra Señora de Candelaria' (1594)
John Mercer, 'The Canary Islanders: Their Prehistory, Conquest and Survival' (1980)
David Abulafia, 'The Discovery of Mankind: Atlantic Encounters in the Age of Columbus' (2008)
